lotl.label: What's Going On With the Jays
lotl.body: Toronto's bats went ice-cold again, mustering just one measly run in a 2-1 gut-punch loss to the Brewers on Thursday — that's back-to-back 2-1 defeats after splitting the series opener, leaving the Blue Jays at 7-11, fourth in the AL East, and four games back amid a two-game skid. Rookie Brandon Sproat gutted out a career-high 6 2/3 innings for his first quality start, but the bullpen faltered when Milwaukee laid down three straight bunts in the seventh to plate the go-ahead run via Garrett Mitchell, stranding Toronto's tying threat at third. No major injuries surfaced, but this road futility — now 1-5 away — screams urgency for Vladimir Guerrero Jr. and the lineup to awaken from their Milwaukee malaise. The Jays aren't buried yet, but the early trajectory demands a reset. They trek to Arizona tonight for a 8:40 ET tilt against the Diamondbacks, with Eric Lauer (1-2, 7.82 ERA) on the hill versus Michael Soroka's hot 3-0 start — win there, or the AL East hunt turns grim fast.

the_latest.headline: Jays fell to Brewers 1–2
the_latest.dek: Full game recap and box score.

lotl.label: What's Going On With the Raptors
lotl.body: The Raptors are standing at the precipice—5th seed, 46-36 record, and the Cleveland Cavaliers loom like a storm system rolling in from the west. Game One tips Saturday at 1 p.m. on Prime Video, and everyone from the talking heads to the oddsmakers has already written Toronto's obituary, penciling in a five- or six-game series exit. The disrespect stings, but it also clarifies things. This roster knows what it needs to prove. Collin Murray-Boyles, the young forward still finding his footing in his first playoff run, has been studying Draymond Green’s defensive blueprint—a sign the Raptors understand they’ll need to outthink a Cavaliers team built on execution and depth. The numbers are brutal: since January, Toronto won 26 games but managed only three victories against top-10 net-rating teams, and the offense sputtered to a 27th-ranked rating in those matchups. That’s the story the tape tells. Saturday’s opener will reveal whether this team has one more gear, or whether the regular season’s careful construction crumbles under playoff pressure.

lotl.label: What's Going On With the Commanders
lotl.body: Six days. That’s all that stands between the Washington Commanders and their chance to reshape a 5-12 season that left them third in the NFC East, nine games behind the division leader. General manager Adam Peters has been crystal clear about his intentions: the Commanders are "more likely than not" staying at No. 7 overall rather than trading back, signaling confidence that elite talent will still be available when they pick on April 23 in Pittsburgh. Peters hosted more than 20 top prospects at a Top Golf event this week—names like Arvell Reese, Sonny Styles, David Bailey, and Mansoor Delane—each one capable of anchoring a defense that desperately needs reinforcement. The mock drafts are all over the map at seven. Edge rusher, linebacker, safety, cornerback—the Commanders have options, and Peters isn’t sweating it. "There’s not too many guys that aren’t scheme fits," he said. The real work begins next week. Watch for whether Peters sticks to his word and pulls the trigger at seven, or whether a trade offer proves too tempting to pass up.
